A History of Mind
Home
Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

F. W. J. Schelling

1775 – 1854

Nature, freedom, and spirit as the self-unfolding of the absolute.

Schelling does not write a “philosophy of mind” in the modern disciplinary sense. What he gives us is more radical and more difficult: a metaphysics of nature, self-consciousness, soul, freedom, evil, history, mythology, and revelation in which “mind” is never a merely mental item. Mind is a phase of reality’s own self-disclosure. The soul is not a Cartesian immaterial substance added to a body; consciousness is not the first principle of philosophy; and theology is not an optional ornament. In Schelling, nature, unconscious productivity, embodiment, will, freedom, God, and historical revelation all belong to the problem of mind.

My reconstruction is this: a Schellingian philosophy of mind is a genetic, non-reductive, embodied, unconscious-centered, voluntarist, theological philosophy of mindedness, where mind is nature becoming internally luminous, but never becoming fully transparent to itself. Consciousness arises from an unconscious ground; selfhood is not self-grounding; the soul mediates nature and spirit; freedom is not just rational autonomy but the power to order or disorder the principles of one’s being; and theological language names the fact that consciousness depends on a ground it has not posited.

1. Orientation: why “mind” is too narrow a translation

The modern English word “mind” risks compressing several Schellingian terms into one: Geist, Seele, Bewusstsein, Selbstbewusstsein, Gemüt, Verstand, Vernunft, Wille, and sometimes even Natur. “Mind” in the analytic sense often refers to consciousness, cognition, intentionality, representation, phenomenal experience, or the mind-body problem. Schelling’s field is broader: the living relation between nature and spirit, the genesis of consciousness out of unconscious activity, the soul as a mediating and productive power, and the moral-theological drama of freedom. Contemporary scholars are therefore right to warn that Schelling should not simply be treated as if he were offering a ready-made solution to today’s “hard problem of consciousness.” Sebastian Gardner, for example, argues that the relation of consciousness to the material world is not a primary, self-standing problem for Schelling in the way it is for contemporary philosophy of mind; Schelling’s deeper problems are the derivation of the finite world from the absolute, the relation of subjective and objective, and the reality of freedom.

That caveat matters, but it should not make us understate Schelling’s relevance. He is one of the major post-Kantian thinkers who refuses both mechanistic materialism and subjectivist idealism. He tries to think a nature that is not dead matter, a subject that is not detached from nature, an unconscious that is productive rather than merely pathological, and a soul that is neither a ghostly substance nor a reducible epiphenomenon. English, German, French, Spanish, and Portuguese scholarship all converge on the point that Schelling’s thought becomes especially fertile when read across Naturphilosophie, transcendental idealism, the Freedom Essay, the Weltalter project, the Stuttgart lectures, and the late philosophies of mythology and revelation.

The following report treats Schelling’s “philosophy of mind” as an extraction from that whole corpus. It is not a claim that Schelling has one perfectly stable doctrine. He changes, revises, and radicalizes his position. Still, there is a deep continuity: the mind is never an isolated res cogitans; it is always a late, fragile, historically situated achievement of a deeper life-process.

2. The controlling thesis: mind is nature become inward to itself

Schelling’s most famous formula is that nature is visible spirit and spirit invisible nature. German commentary still rightly treats this as the key anti-Cartesian intuition: Schelling wants to overcome the modern division between nature and freedom by showing that nature is implicitly spiritual and that human spirit is the highest stage of natural development.

This is not crude animism, nor simple panpsychism in the contemporary sense. Schelling is not saying that every stone has human-like consciousness. He is saying that nature is not a mere external aggregate of objects. Nature is productivity, polarity, organization, striving, inhibition, and self-formation. Consciousness is the highest product of this dynamic nature, but the lower stages are not meaningless matter; they are unconscious or preconscious configurations of the same real-ideal process that becomes explicit in intelligence.

In the early System of Transcendental Idealism, Schelling states the problem sharply: all knowledge involves the coincidence of objective and subjective; nature is the objective or non-conscious side, while the self or intelligence is the subjective and conscious side. The philosophical task is to explain their concurrence. The completed theory of nature, Schelling says there, would resolve nature into intelligence, and nature’s highest goal is to become object to itself in reason or humanity.

The philosophical consequence is decisive: mind is not added to nature from outside. Mind is nature’s own return into itself. But because this return is achieved only through stages, tensions, inhibitions, and oppositions, consciousness is not the origin of reality. It is a result.

3. Schelling’s conceptual vocabulary

A compact glossary helps avoid major distortions.

Term Function in a Schellingian philosophy of mind Natur Not inert matter, but productive, polar, self-organizing reality. Nature is the objective/unconscious side of the same process that becomes spirit. Geist Spirit or mind in a higher sense: the unity in which opposing principles become conscious of belonging to one essence. In Stuttgart, Geist in a narrower sense is the personal potency of consciousness and will. Seele Soul: a mediating, organic, often suprapersonal principle linking body, nature, spirit, and the divine. In the Stuttgart lectures, it is higher than merely personal spirit. Bewusstsein / Selbstbewusstsein Consciousness and self-consciousness. These are not first principles but achievements whose conditions remain partly unconscious. Unbewusst / das Unbewusste The unconscious. In early Schelling it is productive nature and artistic-genetic activity; in middle Schelling it becomes the dark ground, the sunk decision, the non-transparent basis of personhood. Gemüt Character, heart, affective inwardness, or psychic disposition. In the Stuttgart lectures it is the dark principle of spirit, the unconscious source of drives. Wille Will. By 1809, willing becomes primal being: the deepest ontological category. Mind is fundamentally volitional before it is merely representational. Grund / Existenz Ground and existence. God and human beings have a dark ground of existence that is not identical with their manifest existence. This is crucial for freedom and evil. Potenzen Potencies or powers: structured stages of the real-ideal process. They are not mere concepts but dynamic principles of development. Offenbarung / Mythologie Revelation and mythology. In late Schelling, consciousness is historical and religious before it is purely rational; mythological consciousness is a necessary phase in the history of self-consciousness.

This vocabulary already shows why theology cannot be excluded. “Mind” for Schelling is bound to the problem of creation, fall, freedom, evil, divine life, and the soul’s relation to a ground beyond itself.

4. Early Schelling: Naturphilosophie and the genesis of consciousness

The early Schelling’s philosophy of nature begins from a problem inherited from Kant and Fichte. Kant had separated nature as the domain of lawful necessity from freedom as the domain of moral autonomy. Fichte had radicalized the active subject. Schelling’s question is: how can subjectivity and freedom arise in a natural world without reducing them to mechanism, and without making nature a mere projection of the subject? The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy summarizes the early project well: Schelling’s Naturphilosophie develops a dynamic account of nature against mechanism and attempts to bridge the Kantian gap between nature and freedom.

Nature, for Schelling, is not a finished collection of things. It is productivity. The Stanford account emphasizes that Schelling includes ourselves within nature as part of an ascending series of “potentials,” each structured by polar opposition; nature as productivity cannot be conceived merely as an object because it is the subject of possible real predicates. Spanish scholarship on Schelling’s Naturphilosophie stresses the same point in a useful way: in Schelling’s objective idealism, nature is subject and constructs itself; philosophy of nature is the comprehension of nature’s self-construction, not the arbitrary imposition of subjective categories upon nature.

This gives us the first major thesis for a Schellingian philosophy of mind: mind is continuous with nature, but nature is already more than mechanism. Schelling is not trying to derive mind from matter conceived as dead extension. He is trying to show that what we call matter is itself a stabilized, inhibited, polar product of a deeper productivity. In this sense, the mind-body problem changes shape: it is no longer “How does consciousness emerge from inert matter?” but “How does nature’s unconscious productivity become conscious of itself?”

This is also why Schelling’s speculative physics should not be dismissed simply because its scientific details are obsolete. The important philosophical move is that nature is self-organizing and internally differentiated. Spanish analysis notes Schelling’s continuity between inorganic and organic nature: organic life repeats inorganic dynamics at a higher potency, with sensitivity, irritability, and reproductive drive corresponding to higher forms of magnetism, electricity, and chemical process. The historical physics is dated; the philosophical pattern remains significant: life and mind are not miraculous intrusions into nature, but intensifications of nature’s own dynamic structure.

For philosophy of mind, this suggests a non-reductive naturalism. The mind is natural, but nature is not reducible to externally related particles. Nature has internal dynamism, polarity, organization, and directionality. Schelling’s relevance to contemporary embodied and enactive approaches lies here: he refuses to treat the mind as a private container of representations and refuses to treat nature as a mindless external stage.

5. Transcendental idealism: consciousness is a result, not a beginning

Schelling’s System of Transcendental Idealism gives the complementary route. Naturphilosophie moves from nature to intelligence; transcendental philosophy moves from intelligence to nature. The two are not rival disciplines but mirror-images. In the System, Schelling presents a “history of self-consciousness” in which the transcendental subject comes to itself through stages. The Stanford account states that the System recounts the history of which the transcendental subject is the result, with founding stages that bring material nature into being as “unconscious,” then leading to organic nature, consciousness, and self-consciousness.

This is essential. Self-consciousness is not a punctual, transparent given. It is an achievement that depends on activities it cannot fully appropriate. The I does not simply posit itself in pure daylight. It comes to itself through unconscious production, sensation, intuition, reflection, will, and history. The IEP emphasizes that, for Schelling, the history of self-consciousness reveals an unconscious ground inaccessible to consciousness; the condition of possibility of consciousness remains irreducible to consciousness itself.

This is one of Schelling’s most important contributions to philosophy of mind: self-consciousness is constitutively non-self-transparent. The self is not opaque merely because of empirical ignorance; it is structurally unable to survey the whole genesis that makes it possible. Consciousness is like the visible crest of a deeper productive movement.

Schelling’s critique of reflection is related. Reflection splits subject and object, and then tries to reunite them. But any reflective relation presupposes a prior identity that cannot itself be produced by reflection. The Stanford entry explains this through the mirror example: recognizing oneself in a mirror presupposes a prior non-reflexive familiarity with oneself, otherwise the reflection could be taken as a random object. A complete system based only on reflection is therefore impossible, because it must presuppose what it claims to derive.

For philosophy of mind, this has several consequences. First, consciousness cannot be explained by beginning with conscious representation alone. Second, selfhood includes pre-reflective and unconscious conditions. Third, the subject-object split is derivative. Fourth, the mind is not a self-enclosed spectator but an emergent node in a wider real-ideal process.

6. Art as evidence for the conscious-unconscious structure of mind

In Schelling’s early system, art has a privileged philosophical function because it displays the unity of conscious and unconscious productivity. The artist acts intentionally, but the genuine artwork exceeds intention. Its product contains more than the artist could have rule-governedly planned. The Stanford entry notes Schelling’s claim that reality is a product of the interrelation of conscious and unconscious, and that art shows what discursive philosophy cannot represent externally.

The IEP makes the point similarly: art becomes the “eternal organ and document” of philosophy because the artwork shows the synthesis of nature and freedom, conscious intention and unconscious infinity, without reducing that synthesis to propositions. French scholarship on the soul’s poietic function also stresses this: art arises from an equilibrium between conscious and unconscious activity and makes the latent dynamism of natural activity appear.

This matters for philosophy of mind because it expands the forms of cognition. Schelling is not anti-rational, but he rejects the idea that propositional representation exhausts disclosure. Art, myth, religious consciousness, and bodily-organic experience can disclose structures of mind and world that discursive judgment cannot fully master.

The artwork is therefore not a decorative appendix to Schelling’s philosophy. It is a model of mind itself. Consciousness acts, but its deepest products require unconscious productivity. The self is creative only because it is not fully transparent to itself.

7. Body and soul: against Cartesian dualism

Schelling’s account of the soul is one of the richest and most underused resources for extracting a philosophy of mind. French scholarship is especially helpful here. Jean-Christophe Lemaitre’s study of the “poietic function of the soul” argues that Schelling uses the concept of soul first in relation to the conditions of self-consciousness, where the organicity of the body must be taken seriously, and later in his middle-period anthropology, where the soul mediates the unity of spirit and nature.

Schelling’s anti-Cartesian point is not merely that body and soul interact. That would still accept the basic Cartesian framing. Rather, the body is expressive and organic, while the soul is the principle through which the body’s expressive relation to the world becomes inwardly actual. Lemaitre notes Schelling’s metaphor of the body as a “mirror of the universe”: the body reflects the world, but soul is what allows that mirroring to be recognized and interiorized. The soul can even be called “more organic” than any body, because it expresses the body’s capacity to express the world.

This gives us a powerful Schellingian alternative to both dualism and reductive physicalism. The body is not a machine to which a soul is added; the soul is not a detached spectator; the organism is not merely animated matter. The soul is the living interiorization of the body’s world-relation. It is the body’s expressive power raised into inwardness.

French interpretation also emphasizes that, in the years 1797–1800, Schelling is trying to explain consciousness and will in a non-dualist way by taking the organic body seriously. The human being becomes central because it is a natural being in whom the spiritual knows itself.

This suggests that a Schellingian philosophy of mind should be embodied and organicist. Mentality is not located in an abstract ego. It is rooted in organism, affect, expression, and world-relation. But it is not reducible to physiology, because the organism itself is an expression of nature’s deeper productivity.

8. The soul as mediator between nature and spirit

In Schelling, the soul is often the mediating power between body and spirit, nature and freedom, unconscious and consciousness. It is not always terminologically stable across his writings, but a pattern emerges: soul is the living bond through which the human being is both natural and spiritual.

In the middle period, this becomes explicitly anthropological. Lemaitre argues that the human being occupies a central place because, through the soul, it constitutes a unity of body and spirit that preserves a vestige of primordial unity while also bearing the mark of fall or separation. The soul is nostalgic for an original unity but also oriented toward a future reconciliation.

This redemptive dimension is not incidental. In the Freedom Essay and related works, the human being is not just another organism. Humanity is the point at which nature can be interpreted, redeemed, and brought into conscious relation with spirit. Lemaitre notes Schelling’s claim that the human being is “the redeemer of nature,” in an ontologico-moral and hermeneutic sense: nature finds its meaning in and through the human center, but that meaning is bound to the struggle between love/light and contraction/negation.

One must handle this carefully. Schelling is not simply anthropocentric in the modern exploitative sense. His whole project opposes the reduction of nature to an object for manipulation. Spanish scholarship underscores that Schelling criticizes the modern mechanistic conception of nature as object constituted, available, or manipulable by the subject; he does not reject empirical science as such, but rejects the metaphysical reduction of nature to mechanism.

The human being is central because it is where nature’s meaning can become inwardly disclosed, not because nature is worthless without human domination. This has obvious importance for ecological philosophy of mind: consciousness is not a sovereign subject over nature, but nature’s own risky elevation into freedom.

9. The unconscious: from productive nature to dark ground

Schelling is one of the crucial philosophical ancestors of the modern concept of the unconscious. But his unconscious is not simply Freud’s repressed unconscious. It has several layers.

In early Schelling, the unconscious is productive. Nature is unconscious productivity; artistic creation depends on unconscious activity; self-consciousness is generated by processes that it cannot make fully conscious. McGrath’s work on Schelling and the unconscious notes that early Schelling and the Romantics constructed the unconscious to overcome the modern split between subjectivity and nature, mind and body.

In the middle Schelling, the unconscious becomes darker and more existential. It is not only productive creativity but also the ground of character, decision, freedom, and evil. McGrath summarizes the later development as a modification of the unconscious to include the decision to be oneself, which must sink beneath consciousness in order to ground creative and personal acts. He also stresses that Schelling’s unconscious is not merely repression; it is a condition of life and love.

This is one of the sharpest points for philosophy of mind. Schelling does not think the person is fully constituted by explicit deliberation. The self has a ground beneath consciousness. Some fundamental orientation of the person precedes or exceeds reflective choice. Yet this ground is not simply external determinism. It is bound to freedom, character, and will.

In contemporary terms, Schelling offers neither pure compatibilism nor libertarian voluntarism in a simple sense. He is trying to think a grounded freedom: freedom that is not mechanically caused, but also not an arbitrary punctual choice. The person’s conscious acts express a deeper configuration of will, character, and ground.

10. The Freedom Essay: will as primal being

The decisive shift occurs in the 1809 Freedom Essay. Schelling’s concern is no longer only how nature becomes consciousness, but how freedom, evil, and personality are possible within a system. The Stanford entry notes that the middle-period Weltalter project attempts to explain the emergence of an intelligible world while coming to terms with mind’s relation to matter and avoiding Spinozist fatalism.

The famous formula from the Freedom Essay is that willing is primal being. Stanford quotes Schelling’s claim that, in the last and highest instance, there is no being but willing; willing bears predicates such as groundlessness, eternity, independence of time, and self-affirmation. German dissertation research on Schelling’s determination of the human being likewise stresses that willing becomes the highest central category and that without willing there is no being.

This is a radical reorientation of philosophy of mind. Cognition is not primary. Representation is not primary. Even consciousness is not primary. Will is ontologically deeper than cognition. Mind is not first a knowing subject but a willing, striving, affectively charged being.

This aligns Schelling with later voluntarist and depth-psychological traditions, but he is not reducible to Schopenhauer or Freud. Schelling’s will is not merely blind striving. It is internally polarized: contraction and expansion, ground and existence, self-will and universal will, darkness and light. The task of mind is not to eliminate the dark ground but to order it.

11. Ground and existence: why theology enters the philosophy of mind

The Freedom Essay’s theological ontology is indispensable. Schelling distinguishes between God’s existence and the ground of God’s existence. The ground is “nature in God,” inseparable from God but not God considered absolutely. Stanford summarizes this structure: the real, taking the form of material nature, is “in God” but is not God absolutely; it is the ground of God’s existence, nature in God, inseparable from God but different from Him.

This distinction is not a speculative distraction. It is the model for human personality. In both God and the human being there is a difference between dark ground and manifest existence. The human being has an unconscious, dark principle and a conscious principle, which must somehow be identical.

The philosophy of mind extracted from this is profound: a person is not identical with transparent consciousness. A person has a ground. Consciousness is existence, manifestation, light; but it depends on a darker, more archaic, non-transparent basis. This basis is not evil as such. It is the condition of life, individuality, and freedom. Evil appears when the ground is not subordinated to the higher unity of love or universal will.

Schelling’s theology thus gives him a metaphysics of personality. A person is not a simple rational subject. A person is a dynamic unity of principles: dark selfhood and luminous universality, contraction and expansion, individuality and love. The mind is the site where these principles can be rightly ordered or perversely inverted.

12. Evil as a philosophy of mind problem

Schelling’s treatment of evil is a decisive part of his moral psychology. He rejects the idea that evil is merely privation, weakness, sensibility, animality, or ignorance. Evil is a positive possibility rooted in freedom. The IEP emphasizes that, in the Freedom Essay, freedom is not the rational subject’s mastery over sensuous nature but the capacity to do evil; the essay attempts to formulate a system of freedom without reducing freedom to rational necessity.

Michelle Kosch’s analysis of Schelling’s moral psychology is especially useful. She argues that the Freedom Essay presupposes an account of empirical moral agency and that Schelling treats evil as an observable phenomenon, not merely a theological abstraction. She also stresses that evil is not just failure of reason to master sensible inclination; in Schelling’s context, evil is itself a spiritual phenomenon.

This is an extraordinary claim for philosophy of mind. Evil belongs not to the lower animal part alone but to the structure of spirit. Animals have drives, but they do not have the separation of principles needed for evil. Human beings can invert the order of principles: self-will can try to occupy the place of the universal. In Kosch’s summary of the Stuttgart lectures, the capacity for good and evil is rooted in the will’s two-sidedness: an individual/self-will side and a universal or ideal side.

This means that moral pathology is not simply irrationality. It may involve intelligence, understanding, and will. Schelling anticipates a point later depth psychology and existential psychology will develop in other terms: the mind can be brilliant and disordered, conscious and self-deceived, intelligent and evil. The problem is not cognition alone but the ordering of the whole psychic-spiritual being.

13. Stuttgart lectures: Gemüt, Geist, Seele

The Stuttgart Private Lectures are central for reconstructing Schelling’s psychology. Here Schelling distinguishes Gemüt, Geist, and Seele as essential potencies of the human being. A German summary of the Stuttgart lectures notes this tripartite structure and reports Schelling’s striking distinction: spirit knows, but soul is science; spirit can be good because it is capable of evil, while soul is goodness itself; spirit is beautiful, but soul is beauty itself.

Kosch’s reading gives a precise philosophical formulation. In the Stuttgart lectures, character or Gemüt is the dark principle of spirit, the unconscious source of drives. Geist in the narrower sense is the truly personal element and the true potency of consciousness; unconscious desires become conscious in Geist and become will. Soul, by contrast, is the truly divine in the human being and impersonal.

This yields a layered model of mind:

First, Gemüt is the affective-characterological ground. It is dark, drive-like, unconscious, and individualizing. It gives the person depth and orientation, but also danger.

Second, Geist in the narrower sense is personal consciousness and will. It is where desire becomes conscious, where understanding operates, and where freedom becomes practically actual.

Third, Seele is the higher, divine, impersonal principle. It is not “impersonal” because it is subhuman, but because it is above egoic self-will. It is the principle of love, goodness, and reconciliation.

This tripartite structure prevents two errors. Against rationalism, Schelling denies that the conscious rational subject is the whole person. Against reductive psychologism, he denies that the dark affective ground is the highest truth of the person. The human being is an unstable mediation between dark character, personal spirit, and divine soul.

14. Soul above spirit: a surprising Schellingian thesis

One of the most important and easily missed Schellingian claims is that spirit is not simply the highest human faculty. In the Stuttgart lectures, soul is higher than spirit. Kosch notes that Schelling says spirit, because it is capable of illness, error, sin, and evil, cannot be the highest; soul is highest because it is the incorruptible link to the divine.

Recent German dissertation work develops this point in detail. It notes that, for Schelling, a witty or spirited person can be soulless; the soul is divine, unpersonal, goodness itself, and only through soul is connection with God possible. Psychic illness appears when the connection to the soul is interrupted, and a split between understanding and soul can produce madness.

This is philosophically rich. “Spirit” as personal intelligence is ambiguous. It can know, calculate, invent, dominate, and even rationalize evil. Soul is not mere consciousness but the deeper integrative principle that binds the person to love, God, nature, and wholeness. A philosophy of mind drawn from Schelling must therefore distinguish intelligence from soulfulness.

This also gives a Schellingian account of pathology. Mental disorder is not simply neurological malfunction, though Schelling is not denying embodiment. Nor is it merely moral failure. It can be a disjunction in the internal order of Gemüt, Geist, and Seele: drives become disconnected from higher integration; understanding separates from soul; personal spirit becomes autonomous without love.

15. Feeling, affect, and the non-rational depth of mind

Schelling’s model is not intellectualist. Feeling, longing, desire, drive, melancholy, fear, and love are ontologically significant. They are not secondary “emotions” attached to cognition. The dark ground is affectively charged; will emerges through desire; the soul’s highest principle is love.

German research on the Stuttgart lectures notes that feeling is the highest stage in Gemüt and that the human being should not be understood merely as a rational subject but as a soul-subject. It also argues that the dark real side should not be repressed but organically accepted.

This is one of Schelling’s most contemporary features. His philosophy of mind is affective and depth-oriented. Conscious cognition is not sovereign; it is rooted in drives and moods. But unlike some later reductions of psyche to drive, Schelling insists that drives require integration by soul and love. The dark ground is not to be destroyed, because without it there is no life, individuality, or creativity. But if it becomes dominant, the person is disordered.

16. The Weltalter: the soul’s co-knowledge of creation

The unfinished Ages of the World deepens Schelling’s philosophy of mind by turning philosophy into an anamnetic, historical, almost prophetic discipline. In the Weltalter fragment, Schelling says that true science is not a mere sequence of concepts but the development of a living, real being. The “primordially living” develops freely from its own drive and will, yet lawfully.

Most strikingly, Schelling writes that the human soul has a Mitwissenschaft of creation, a co-knowledge or con-science of creation, because it is drawn from the source of things. This knowledge is not simply explicit information. The memory of things sleeps in the soul as a darkened and forgotten image, not wholly extinguished.

This has major consequences. The soul is not merely individual psychology. It bears an obscure relation to the world-process. Philosophy is not merely discursive analysis but an awakening of the soul’s latent memory of creation. Schelling even describes philosophical inquiry as an inner dialogue between two beings in us: one questioning and one answering, one seeking knowledge and one that knows without knowing that it knows.

A Schellingian philosophy of mind therefore includes anamnesis: consciousness is historical memory of nature, creation, and world-genesis. This is not memory in the empirical autobiographical sense. It is the soul’s latent participation in the world’s coming-to-be.

17. Knowledge, inwardness, and the necessity of mediation

The Weltalter also gives an epistemology of inwardness. Schelling says that everything, even what is external by nature, must first become inward before it can be outwardly or objectively presented. The monuments of natural history remain dead until the sequence of nature’s acts and productions has become inward in the human being.

This does not mean subjective projection. It means that understanding nature requires a correspondence between nature’s genesis and the inner life of the knower. The knower is part of the same process that nature expresses externally. In Spanish terms, philosophy follows nature’s genesis from within; empirical science deals with products, while philosophy tries to grasp productivity.

This is not anti-scientific. Schelling’s philosophy of nature insists on relation to empirical knowledge, even though his balance between speculation and experiment is controversial. Spanish scholarship notes that, for Schelling, propositions of philosophy of nature are derived a priori but must also be propositions of experience; philosophy systematizes and grounds, while empirical science supplies content and confirmation.

For philosophy of mind, this means that cognition is not passive representation. Knowing requires inner participation, constructive synthesis, and a living relation between knower and known. Mind is world-involving.

18. Judgment, identity, and difference

Schelling’s logic also matters for philosophy of mind. In the Weltalter, he rejects the simplistic view that identity means flat sameness. In judgment, subject and predicate are not simply identical as terms; rather, there is a deeper bond or copula through which the same underlying x can be A and B under different relations.

This helps explain his whole philosophy of nature and mind. Nature and spirit are not identical as undifferentiated sameness. They are identical as two expressions or potencies of one underlying reality. Conscious and unconscious are not the same, but they are not externally unrelated. Body and soul are not two substances, but neither is one simply reducible to the other.

A Schellingian philosophy of mind is therefore neither dualist nor monist in a flattening sense. It is an identity-in-difference theory: mind and nature belong to one process, but their difference is real and productive.

19. Late Schelling: positive philosophy and the facticity of mind

Late Schelling’s distinction between negative and positive philosophy is crucial. Negative philosophy concerns the “what” of being—essence, conceptual structure, possibility. Positive philosophy concerns the “that” of being—actual existence, facticity, the fact that something exists at all. The IEP summarizes this distinction clearly: positive philosophy begins from the actuality or facticity of existence, which cannot be grasped merely as a logical category or deduced from a concept.

The Stanford entry gives the broader anti-Hegelian point: Schelling argues that human reason cannot explain its own existence and cannot encompass itself and its other within a completed rational system. We cannot begin with reason alone; we must begin from the contingency of being, with reason as only one aspect of being.

This is a late transformation of the earlier claim that consciousness is not self-grounding. Now the point becomes historical and existential. The mind cannot deduce itself from pure thought. It finds itself already existing, already in a world, already after nature, already within history, already shaped by myth, religion, and contingency.

For philosophy of mind, this means that the subject is factical. It is not merely a transcendental structure. It is thrown—though Schelling does not use Heidegger’s vocabulary—into a world whose existence it did not posit. This is why religion becomes philosophically relevant: religion is the relation of consciousness to a being not posited by consciousness itself. Stanford explicitly links late Schelling’s philosophy of religion to the reference of consciousness to what it has not posited.

20. Mythological consciousness

Late Schelling’s philosophy of mythology is not a merely antiquarian study of myths. It is a theory of consciousness. Peter Dews summarizes Schelling’s “God-positing consciousness” and the mythological process as a dynamic interaction of potencies that unfolds in the human mind in a compulsive and dream-like manner. Dews also notes that Schelling’s account anticipates psychoanalytic themes, including dreamlike and neurotic structures.

This gives us another layer of philosophy of mind: consciousness is not initially secular, rational, and autonomous. It passes through mythological forms. Myth is not simply false belief; it is a necessary symbolic-dramatic form in which consciousness processes its relation to divine powers, nature, fear, dependency, and liberation.

This is highly relevant to contemporary philosophy of mind if we broaden “mind” to include symbolic formation, collective imagination, and religious consciousness. Schelling sees human consciousness as historically structured by powers it does not invent at will. Mythology is not mere subjective fantasy; it expresses the deep history of consciousness.

21. Revelation and philosophy: theology is not an accidental supplement

Schelling’s late Philosophy of Revelation should not be treated as an embarrassment or a distraction. It is controversial, but it belongs to his attempt to think actuality, freedom, and history beyond purely logical philosophy. In the Berlin lectures, Schelling explicitly says philosophy must concern itself with revelation, while also rejecting the idea that the philosophy of revelation is simply drawn from or grounded in revelation.

German theological scholarship emphasizes that Schelling did not intend the Philosophy of Revelation as ordinary dogmatic theology. He says his concern is not doctrine or dogmatics but the matter, the object, the reality at issue. Yet this object is concrete Christianity, not an abstract concept of revelation.

For philosophy of mind, the relevant point is that consciousness cannot complete itself through immanent reason alone. Revelation names a historical event or disclosure that cannot be deduced from consciousness but can transform consciousness. This is continuous with Schelling’s lifelong insistence that consciousness depends on what it has not posited.

The same German discussion stresses that the Philosophy of Revelation becomes a system of freedom, not a system of necessity, and therefore cannot lead to closed absolute knowledge. Finite human knowledge remains unfinished; thinking passes continually from one thing to another.

The theological implication is strong: mind is finite, dependent, and historically addressed. It does not possess the absolute by conceptual mastery. It receives, responds, resists, interprets, and is transformed.

22. The world soul, Trinity, and consciousness inside nature

Recent theological interpretation of late Schelling, especially in McGrath’s work, develops the relation between world soul, nature, Spirit, and revelation. McGrath argues that, for late Schelling, fallen consciousness is not merely subjective: consciousness is “the inside of nature,” so an alteration in consciousness is also a change in the real. He further interprets the Spirit as a cosmic potency that quickens matter into life and becomes the telos of an unconsciously unfolding material order.

This is a theologically charged reading, and not all interpreters will accept it in full. But it brings out a genuine Schellingian tendency: mind, nature, and divine history are internally related. The history of consciousness is not merely psychological. It is part of the history of nature and, in late Schelling, part of the history of God’s self-revelation.

McGrath also emphasizes that Schelling does not demonize matter in a straightforward Gnostic way; even where Schelling’s theology renders God vulnerable to evil, his lifelong reverence for nature prevents him from treating material creation as evil in itself. This is important because Schelling’s theological philosophy of mind is not anti-body or anti-nature. The body and nature remain grounds of spirit.

In another late-theological formulation, McGrath writes that nature is the ground of freedom, hierarchically ordered but continuous; one Spirit moves through human and non-human beings, from pre-personal and unconscious forms toward personal and conscious individuation. Whether one accepts this as theology or reads it as speculative metaphysics, it confirms the basic Schellingian principle: consciousness is not outside nature; it is nature’s inward history.

23. Extracted systematic philosophy of mind

We can now formulate the comprehensive philosophy of mind latent in Schelling.

23.1 Ontology: real-ideal identity without reduction

Mind and nature are not two substances. Nor is mind reducible to matter as conceived by mechanism. The real and the ideal are different potencies of one process. Nature is unconscious spirit; spirit is conscious or inwardized nature. The correct model is not dualism, not reductive materialism, and not subjective idealism. It is dynamic identity-in-difference.

23.2 Genesis: consciousness emerges through stages

Consciousness is not primitive. It emerges from unconscious productivity through stages: inorganic polarity, organism, sensation, productive intuition, reflection, will, self-consciousness, art, morality, myth, and religion. This genealogy is speculative, not empirical neuroscience, but it offers a philosophical account of why consciousness is both natural and irreducible.

23.3 Embodiment: soul is the inwardness of organism

The body is not a machine. It is an expressive organism, a microcosmic mirror of the world. Soul is not an immaterial substance separable in Cartesian fashion, but the living interiorization of the body’s expressive relation to world. The soul actualizes the body’s ideality.

23.4 The unconscious: condition, not merely repression

The unconscious is productive, ontological, and moral. It is nature’s productivity, the hidden condition of self-consciousness, the source of artistic creation, the dark ground of character, and the sunk basis of personhood. It is not merely a storehouse of repressed contents. It is the condition of life, love, creativity, and freedom.

23.5 Consciousness: real but non-self-grounding

Consciousness is a genuine achievement, but it cannot ground itself. It depends on pre-reflective identity, unconscious production, embodiment, drives, history, and a factical world it did not posit. It is therefore finite and structurally incomplete.

23.6 Will: more fundamental than representation

Schelling’s mature philosophy places will beneath cognition. The person is not first a spectator but a willing being. Desire, longing, contraction, self-affirmation, love, and freedom are more basic than detached representation. Mind is volitional-affective before it is theoretical.

23.7 Personhood: a unity of dark ground and light

Personhood requires both ground and existence, dark individuality and universal light. Without the dark ground, there is no individuality or life. Without the higher principle, there is disorder. The person is the dynamic ordering of principles, not a simple rational atom.

23.8 Freedom: the power to order or invert principles

Freedom is not merely choice among options. It is the capacity to subordinate self-will to universal will or to invert that order. Evil is not mere ignorance or animal impulse; it is spiritual disorder, the elevation of self-will over the whole. Moral psychology is therefore central to philosophy of mind.

23.9 Soul: the higher integrative principle

Soul is not identical with personal consciousness. In the Stuttgart lectures, soul is higher than spirit because spirit can err, sin, and become sick, while soul is the incorruptible link to the divine. A complete philosophy of mind must distinguish intelligence from soulfulness, consciousness from wisdom, personality from love.

23.10 Art, myth, and religion: non-propositional disclosures

Not all truth is propositional representation. Art discloses the unity of conscious and unconscious. Myth discloses the historical-dreamlike formation of consciousness. Religion discloses consciousness’s relation to what it has not posited. These are not irrational residues but necessary modes of mind’s relation to its ground.

23.11 Pathology: disordered relation among psychic principles

Mental and moral pathology arise when the relation among Gemüt, Geist, and Seele is disordered: drives detach from integration, understanding separates from soul, self-will dominates universal will, or spirit becomes brilliant but soulless. Schelling therefore offers a speculative psychopathology, not merely a theory of cognition.

23.12 History: mind is historical through and through

Mind is not only individual consciousness. It is historical consciousness: nature’s history, the soul’s latent memory of creation, mythology, revelation, and the unfinished development of freedom. Consciousness must understand itself historically because it is not self-originating.

24. Schelling and the contemporary “hard problem”

Schelling is relevant to contemporary philosophy of mind, but not because he directly solves the hard problem of qualia. Gardner’s warning is apt: Schelling would likely regard the attempt to isolate “brain states” and “subjective mental processes” as two independent items needing external connection as already misconceived. The mind-body relation, for Schelling, is a derivative instance of the more basic real-ideal structure of nature itself.

A Schellingian response to the hard problem would not be: “Here is how neural firings produce qualia.” It would be: the framing presupposes a mechanistic concept of nature and an isolated concept of consciousness, then wonders how to reconnect what it has abstractly separated. Schelling’s alternative is to reconceive nature from the beginning as productive, polar, and proto-spiritual, and to reconceive consciousness as a late intensification of nature’s inwardness.

This does not replace neuroscience. Schelling does not give a modern empirical theory of the brain. Rather, he gives a metaphysical framework in which brain, organism, affect, consciousness, and world are not treated as externally related. That is why he is closer to process philosophy, embodied cognition, enactivism, depth psychology, and non-reductive naturalism than to classical Cartesian dualism or reductive physicalism.

25. Schelling’s relation to panpsychism, emergentism, and idealism

Schelling can look like a panpsychist because he treats nature as unconscious spirit and sees intelligence implicitly present in nature. But he is not a standard panpsychist if that means every entity has its own tiny phenomenal consciousness. Nature’s lower products are not conscious subjects. They are unconscious products or potencies of a productivity that culminates in consciousness.

He can also look like an emergentist because mind emerges from nature. Gardner notes that Schelling is, in some sense, a non-reductionist and emergentist who thinks mind emerges from matter and depends on embodiment, but he cautions that everything turns on the phrase “in some sense.” Schelling’s “matter” is not mechanistic matter, and his emergence is not a brute appearance of consciousness out of the non-conscious; it is the unfolding of an already real-ideal nature.

He is also an idealist, but not a subjective idealist. The object is not merely constructed by the ego. Nature is productive subject in its own right. Schelling’s idealism is therefore better called objective idealism, real-idealism, or speculative naturalism, depending on context.

26. Theological importance: why God matters to mind

Theological language in Schelling does at least four philosophical jobs.

First, it gives an account of ground. Consciousness depends on what it does not posit. In theological terms, creaturely mind depends on God, but God himself is not a static concept; divine life includes ground, existence, opposition, and revelation.

Second, theology gives an account of personality. God is not an abstract One but living, self-revealing personality. Human personhood mirrors this structure in finite form: ground, existence, selfhood, love, freedom.

Third, theology gives an account of evil. Evil is not merely error, sensibility, or privation. It is a real possibility of freedom, grounded in the inversion of principles. This requires a metaphysics deeper than moral rationalism.

Fourth, theology gives an account of history. Consciousness is not fulfilled by timeless conceptual knowledge. It is transformed through mythology, revelation, fall, redemption, and future reconciliation. Late Schelling’s positive philosophy is therefore inseparable from historical consciousness.

The theological elements are risky and often heterodox. They cannot be casually translated into secular psychology without loss. But treating them as distractions would badly misread Schelling. They are part of his answer to the question: how can finite consciousness exist, suffer, err, create, remember, love, and become free?

27. Schellingian philosophy of mind in one formula

A concise formula would be:

Mind is nature’s self-inwardization through organism, soul, unconscious productivity, will, and history, under the condition of a ground that consciousness cannot posit and a freedom that can either reconcile or invert the principles of its being.

Or, more simply:

Consciousness is not the foundation of the soul; the soul is the living, unconscious-conscious mediation through which nature, spirit, freedom, and God become inwardly related.

28. Principal strengths of Schelling’s model

Schelling avoids the deadlock between Cartesian dualism and reductive materialism. He gives a naturalistic account of mind only by transforming the concept of nature. He gives a spiritual account of mind without detaching spirit from embodiment. He gives an account of consciousness that includes the unconscious without reducing the unconscious to pathology. He gives a moral psychology in which evil is intelligible as a disorder of freedom rather than a mere cognitive mistake. And he gives a theological account in which finitude, facticity, history, and revelation are not accidental to mind but constitutive of its situation.

He is also unusually helpful for thinking creativity. Art, myth, and religion are not decorative; they disclose the limits of reflective consciousness. In this regard, Schelling remains powerful for aesthetics, depth psychology, religious philosophy, ecological thought, and post-Cartesian philosophy of mind.

29. Principal difficulties

Schelling’s natural science is historically dated. His speculative use of magnetism, electricity, chemistry, and organism cannot be straightforwardly mapped onto contemporary physics or biology. His systematic vocabulary changes across periods. His theology is heterodox and contested. His late philosophy of revelation can seem to blur philosophy and Christian theology, though Schelling himself insists that philosophy of revelation is not simply dogmatics.

There is also a methodological danger: one can make Schelling too contemporary by translating him into enactivism, psychoanalysis, panpsychism, or process metaphysics too quickly. Gardner’s caution remains important: Schelling’s own project is not identical with contemporary philosophy of mind.

Still, these difficulties do not undermine the extraction. They define its conditions. Schelling is most useful when read neither as an obsolete Romantic physicist nor as a proto-analytic philosopher of consciousness, but as a speculative thinker of nature, soul, unconsciousness, freedom, and divine-historical facticity.

30. Final synthesis

Schelling’s philosophy of mind begins by rejecting the premise that mind and nature are externally related. Nature is already implicitly spiritual because it is productive, polar, dynamic, and self-organizing. Mind is nature’s return into itself, but this return is never complete transparency. Consciousness is late, dependent, and partial. Self-consciousness rests on an unconscious ground. The body is not a machine but an expressive organism. The soul is the living mediation between body, nature, spirit, and God. Will is deeper than representation. Freedom is the power to order or disorder the principles of one’s being. Evil is a real spiritual possibility. Art reveals the unity of conscious and unconscious; myth reveals the dreamlike history of consciousness; revelation reveals consciousness’s dependence on what it did not posit.

The most comprehensive Schellingian philosophy of mind, then, is not a theory of “mental states.” It is a theory of the living, embodied, unconscious-conscious, morally dangerous, historically developing, theologically grounded soul.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
